content.text: QUICK REFERENCE GUIDE FOR TEACHERS If a student discloses abuse to you:

steps: ✅ Listen calmly, don't interrupt
✅ Believe the student
✅ Say: "Thank you for telling me. I'm here to help."
✅ Report to DSL immediately (same day)
✅ Complete Concern Form (Appendix 9-A)
❌ DON'T promise confidentiality
❌ DON'T investigate yourself Emergency? Call DSL: [phone number] DSL not available? Call DDSL: [phone number] Immediate danger? Call 112 (Police/Medical) For full details, see Section 9.8 (page 37)

content.text: To establish a comprehensive framework that protects all students from harm, promotes their well-being, and creates a culture of safety across all Eduversal Partner Schools. This section ensures compliance with Indonesian child protection laws, aligns with CIS/WASC standards, and reflects Cambridge Centre safeguarding requirements.
Eduversal Partner Schools are committed to safeguarding and promoting the welfare of all children and young people. We recognize that:

items: Every child has the right to protection from abuse, neglect, exploitation, and harm
Every adult in our school community has a responsibility to act on concerns
Prevention, early intervention, and timely response are essential to effective safeguarding
Partnership with families strengthens our ability to keep children safe
Continuous learning and improvement ensure our practices remain robust
content.title: Policy Statement

content.text: This policy applies to all employees, board members, contractors, volunteers, and visitors across all campuses and school-sponsored activities (on-site, off-site, residential, trips, and online).

steps: Safe Recruitment: We hire only individuals suitable to work with children, using rigorous screening processes
Training and Awareness: All staff receive regular safeguarding training; students and parents learn about safety
Culture of Vigilance: Everyone knows how to recognize and report concerns
Prompt and Effective Response: Concerns are taken seriously, documented, and acted upon
Support for Victims: Students who experience harm receive compassionate, professional support
Partnership with Authorities: We work collaboratively with Indonesian child protection agencies and law enforcement
content.title: Core Commitments

items: All students enrolled in Eduversal Partner Schools (ages 3-18)
Particular attention to vulnerable groups: students with disabilities, EAL learners, boarders, students experiencing family difficulties
content.title: Who is Protected?

content.text: Child/Young Person: Any individual under 18 years of age (consistent with Indonesian law and international standards). Abuse: Actions or failures to act that cause harm or risk of harm to a child’s physical, emotional, or psychological well-being. Categories include:

steps: Physical Abuse: Hitting, slapping, shaking, burning, or other physical harm
Emotional/Psychological Abuse: Persistent emotional maltreatment (humiliation, rejection, threats, witnessing domestic violence)
Sexual Abuse: Any sexual activity with a child, including non-contact abuse (exposure to sexual content, grooming)
Neglect: Failure to provide adequate food, shelter, medical care, supervision, or emotional support
Peer-on-Peer Abuse: Bullying (including cyberbullying), physical violence, sexual harassment, hazing, initiation rituals
Online Harms: Cyberbullying, grooming, exposure to inappropriate content, sexting, online exploitation
content.title: Key Definitions

content.text: Safeguarding: Proactive measures to prevent harm and promote well-being (broader than child protection, which focuses on responding to abuse). Designated Safeguarding Lead (DSL): Senior staff member with primary responsibility for coordinating safeguarding, managing disclosures, and liaising with external agencies. Deputy DSL (DDSL): Staff member(s) trained to support the DSL and act in their absence. Acronyms Used in This Document:

steps: DSL \= Designated Safeguarding Lead
DDSL \= Deputy Designated Safeguarding Lead
SCR \= Single Central Register
KPAI \= Indonesian Child Protection Commission
P2TP2A \= Local Integrated Service Center
CPD \= Continuous Professional Development
PSHE \= Personal, Social, Health Education
PPKn \= Pancasila and Citizenship Education

items: Law No. 35 of 2014 (Amendment to Law No. 23 of 2002 on Child Protection)
Law No. 17 of 2016 (Child Protection in the Digital Environment)
Ministry of Education regulations on school safety and anti-violence
content.title: Indonesian Child Protection Law

items: Mandatory reporting of suspected abuse to authorities (police, social services)
Criminal penalties for failure to report known abuse
Code of Conduct for all school personnel
Anti-violence and anti-bullying policies
Safe school environment standards
Komisi Perlindungan Anak Indonesia (KPAI) \- Indonesian Child Protection Commission
Pusat Pelayanan Terpadu Pemberdayaan Perempuan dan Anak (P2TP2A) \- Local integrated service centers
Police (Unit Perlindungan Perempuan dan Anak) \- Women and Children Protection Unit

content.text: A. UU No. 35/2014 \- Key Provisions for Schools Mandatory Reporting (Pasal 76):

items: Legal Obligation: Every person who knows, sees, or experiences child abuse MUST report within 24 hours
Criminal Penalties for Failure to Report:
Prison: Up to 5 years
Fine: Up to Rp 100,000,000
Protected Reporters: Those reporting in good faith cannot be sued (civil immunity)
content.title: Practical Implementation Guide

steps: Physical Violence (Pasal 76C): Hitting, slapping, kicking → Report to Police Unit PPA
Sexual Abuse (Pasal 76D/E): Any sexual activity with child under 18 → Report immediately to police \+ P2TP2A
Neglect (Pasal 77): Failure to provide basic needs → Report to Dinas Sosial \+ P2TP2A
Exploitation (Pasal 76I): Child labor, forcing begging → Report to police \+ Dinas Sosial
Online Exploitation (UU 17/2016): Cyberbullying, grooming, child pornography → Report to police cybercrime unit \+ Kemkominfo

content.text: School-Specific Requirements: Permendikbud No. 82/2015 (Anti-Violence in Education):

items: Schools must have written safeguarding policy (✓ This document)
Annual training for ALL staff (minimum 4 hours)
Student-friendly reporting mechanism (suggestion box, trusted adult system)
Anti-bullying curriculum integrated into PPKN
Copy of policy displayed in staff room and reception
Training attendance sheets
Student handbook section on reporting
Lesson plans showing anti-bullying content
24-hour rule is LEGAL REQUIREMENT \- failure is criminal offense
Do NOT delay to "gather more evidence" \- report suspicion, let authorities investigate
Police report number (BAP/LP) must be obtained \- file in secure safeguarding records
content.title: Reporting Flowchart

content.text: Local Agency Contact Templates For Each School to Complete: ![][image3] Instructions for Schools: Each Eduversal Partner School must complete this table with their specific local contacts and update it annually (or whenever contact information changes). Where to Find Contact Information:

steps: Police Unit PPA: Contact your local police station (Polsek/Polres) and ask for the Unit Perlindungan Perempuan dan Anak (PPA) contact person
P2TP2A: Search "[Your City/Province] P2TP2A" or contact your local Dinas Pemberdayaan Perempuan dan Perlindungan Anak (DP3A)
Dinas Sosial: Contact your local government Social Welfare Office
KPAI Regional: Visit www.kpai.go.id for regional office contacts
Hospital with Visum: Identify nearest hospital with forensic/visum et repertum services (typically RSUD or major hospitals)

items: Completed contact table stored in DSL office and Principal's office
Digital copy accessible to all senior leaders
Annual verification that all contacts are current
Staff training includes review of local contacts
Standard 7B: Health and Safety (safeguarding policies and practices)
Standard 3C: School Culture (safe, inclusive environment)

items: Category E: School Culture and Support for Student Personal and Academic Growth (safety, wellbeing, support systems)
Safeguarding policy aligned with UK guidelines (due to Cambridge International affiliation)
Safe recruitment and training for all exam personnel
Secure handling of student data

content.text: UN Convention on the Rights of the Child (UNCRC): Indonesia is a signatory; schools uphold children’s rights to protection, participation, and provision.

items: Approve network-wide Safeguarding Policy and review annually
Ensure adequate resources (staffing, training, systems) for safeguarding
Receive annual safeguarding report from Director of Primary / Secondary Education
Monitor compliance across all partner schools

steps: Overall accountability at school level (as per Indonesian law \- GM is employer representative)
Appoint DSL

steps: Create and maintain culture of safety
Embed safeguarding in curriculum

content.text: Appointment: Typically a Vice Principal (Student Affairs/Wellbeing), Counselor, or senior staff member with:

items: Strong interpersonal and communication skills
Understanding of child development and trauma-informed practice
Ability to work with external agencies (police, social services, medical professionals)
Must be a senior leader with authority to make safeguarding decisions
Recommended: Vice Principal (Student Affairs/Wellbeing) or Head of Pastoral Care
May NOT be: School Culture Coordinator (unless they also hold senior leadership position)
Distinction:DSL \= Safeguarding (abuse, neglect, child protection)
School Culture Coordinator \= Discipline and behavioral management (under DSL oversight for safeguarding-related behavior issues)
content.title: Designated Safeguarding Lead (DSL)

content.text: Core Responsibilities: 1. First Point of Contact:

items: Receive all safeguarding concerns and disclosures
Assess level of risk and urgency
Decide on next steps (internal support, external referral, immediate intervention)
Maintain secure, confidential records of all concerns (Section 9.10)
Coordinate support plans for students at risk
Liaise with external agencies (police, social services, counselors)
Monitor ongoing cases and follow up on actions
Organize induction training for new staff and volunteers
Facilitate annual refresher training for all staff
Provide specialized training for trip leaders, boarding staff, coaches
Review and update Safeguarding Policy annually (in consultation with SLT)
Develop and communicate clear reporting procedures
Ensure all staff know how to access policy and reporting forms
Work with Principal, General Manager, and external agencies
Report serious incidents to Board via Director of Primary / Secondary Education
Coordinate with Indonesian child protection authorities
Review log of low-level concerns for patterns (Section 9.9)
Provide guidance to staff on boundary-appropriate behavior
Initial DSL training (minimum 2 days, accredited program)
Annual refresher training
Access to ongoing professional development (conferences, networks)
DSL appointment letter and safeguarding in job description
Training certificates (DSL-specific)
Case management logs (anonymized summaries for audits)
Annual safeguarding report to GM/Principal

content.text: Purpose: Provide backup for DSL; ensure safeguarding function continues if DSL unavailable. Number: Minimum 2 DDSLs in schools with 200+ students; 1 DDSL in smaller schools. Responsibilities:

items: Same as DSL when acting in their absence
Support DSL with case management (as assigned)
Participate in training and meetings
content.title: Deputy Designated Safeguarding Lead(s) (DDSL)

items: DDSL appointment letters
Training completion records

content.text: Universal Duty to Report: Every adult in the school community must:

items: Recognize signs of abuse, neglect, or distress in students
Report concerns immediately to DSL/DDSL (or directly to authorities if imminent danger)
Not investigate concerns themselves (leave this to DSL and authorities)
Maintain confidentiality (share information only with DSL and on “need to know” basis)
Follow school policies and procedures
Complete mandatory safeguarding training annually

items: Monitor student well-being during lessons and activities
Build trusting relationships that enable disclosures
Teach age-appropriate safety education (online safety, consent, healthy relationships)
Provide therapeutic support to students experiencing trauma or distress
Work closely with DSL on support plans
Facilitate psychoeducation groups (e.g., coping skills, peer conflict resolution)
Enhanced vigilance given 24/7 contact with students
Additional training on managing disclosures in a residential setting
Safeguarding risk assessments for the boarding environment
Complete a safeguarding risk assessment before any off-site activity
Maintain appropriate ratios and supervision
Carry emergency contact information and know how to report concerns remotely
Training attendance records (100% staff completion annually)
Signed Code of Conduct and safeguarding acknowledgment forms
Safeguarding in job descriptions and contracts

content.text: Empowerment and Education: Students are taught:

items: Their rights: To be safe, respected, heard
How to recognize unsafe situations or inappropriate behavior
How to report concerns (trusted adults, reporting systems)
Peer support: Looking out for friends; knowing when to seek help
content.title: Students

items: Primary: Personal safety (body boundaries, saying “no,” trusted adults)
Lower Secondary: Online safety, peer pressure, bullying, consent
Upper Secondary: Healthy relationships, sexual health, mental health awareness
Talk to any trusted adult (teacher, counselor, DSL, parent)
Use anonymous reporting system (e.g., suggestion box, online form)
Contact external helplines (provided in student handbook)

Encouraged to communicate concerns about their child or others

content.text: Purpose: Prevent unsuitable individuals from working with children.

items: Safeguarding responsibilities explicitly stated in all roles
Statement: “This position involves regulated activity with children and requires safeguarding checks.”
content.title: Recruitment Process for All Staff and Volunteers

items: Application form requires full employment history (no gaps unexplained)
Declaration: “Have you ever been convicted of an offense, cautioned, or subject to disciplinary action involving children?”
Consent for background checks
At least one safeguarding question (e.g., “How would you respond if a student disclosed abuse?”)
Assess suitability to work with children (values, attitudes, understanding of boundaries)
Minimum 2 references, including most recent employer
Safeguarding-specific questions: “Is the candidate suitable to work with children? Have there been any concerns about their conduct with children?”
Follow up on ambiguous or concerning responses
Original ID documents (KTP for Indonesian nationals, passport for foreigners)
Work permits (KITAS/KITAP) for expatriate staff (Section 2.9)
Academic credentials verified

content.text: Step 6: Background Checks For Indonesian Nationals:

items: SKCK (Surat Keterangan Catatan Kepolisian) \- Police clearance certificate
Obtained from local police (valid 6 months)
Check for criminal history, including crimes against children
Criminal background check from country of origin (obtained before arrival; required for KITAS application)
International DBS (Disclosure and Barring Service) check if from UK
Equivalent checks from other countries (FBI check for US citizens, AFP check for Australians, etc.)
Renewal required if staff return to home country for extended period and then return to Indonesia
Same checks as paid staff if regular, unsupervised contact with students
Supervised volunteers (e.g., one-time event helpers) may have reduced checks but must sign Code of Conduct

content.text: Step 7: Induction Training Timing: Must be completed BEFORE first day of unsupervised contact with students Role-Specific Training Requirements:

steps: Teachers: Level 1 Fundamental Child Protection Training for Educators (full course, certificate valid 2 years)

Regular Volunteers: Child Protection Training for Volunteers (30-45 minutes, certificate valid 2 years)

Board Members/Senior Leaders: Child Protection Training for Board Members, Owners, and Senior Leaders (1 hour, certificate valid 2 years)

Certificate number
Staff member cannot begin unsupervised duties until verification complete

content.text: Q: What if the student asks me to keep it secret? A: Say: "I want to help you stay safe. I need to share this with [DSL name] who is trained to help. We'll only tell people who need to know to keep you safe." (See Code of Conduct, page 48) Q: What if I'm not 100% sure it's abuse? A: Report it anyway. It's not your job to investigate or be certain. The DSL is trained to assess the situation. Better to report and be wrong than not report and a child suffers. (See Reporting Procedures, page 41) Q: What if the alleged abuser is my colleague/supervisor? A: Report to DSL. If DSL is involved, report to Principal. If Principal involved, report to GM or Eduversal Director of Primary / Secondary Education. (See Whistleblowing, page 63) Q: What if parents get angry at me for reporting? A: You are legally and professionally obligated to report. The school will support you. All reports made in good faith are protected. (See Indonesian Law Requirements, page 8) Q: What if I reported but nothing seems to be happening? A: Follow up with DSL. All concerns must be documented and assessed. You have the right to know the concern was received and is being addressed. (See Low-Level Concerns, page 44) Q: What if I realize I made a mistake and reported unnecessarily? A: This is okay. It's better to report and be wrong than not report. You won't face consequences for good-faith reporting. (See Whistleblowing Protections, page 64)
content.title: Frequently Asked Questions

items: Unexplained bruises, burns, fractures (especially in patterns or recurring)
Flinching when approached or touched
Reluctance to discuss injuries; inconsistent explanations
content.title: Signs and Indicators of Abuse

items: Low self-esteem, excessive fear, anxiety
Withdrawal from friends, activities
Self-harm, eating disorders, substance abuse
Developmental delays (speech, social skills)
Sexualized behavior or language inappropriate for age
Fear of specific individuals or places
Physical symptoms (genital pain, infections, pregnancy)
Sudden changes in behavior (regression, nightmares, avoiding school)
Poor hygiene, inappropriate clothing for the weather
Chronic hunger, scavenging for food
Frequent absences, constantly tired
Untreated medical or dental issues
Bullying: Targeted teasing, exclusion, physical aggression, and spreading rumors
Cyberbullying: Harassment via social media, sharing embarrassing images
Sexual harassment: Unwanted touching, sexual comments, pressuring for images
Fighting, hazing, and initiation rituals
Changes in online behavior (secretive, anxious when using devices)
Receiving messages at unusual times, from unknown contacts
Exposure to inappropriate content
Involvement in risky online activities (sharing personal info, meeting strangers)
Persistent sadness, hopelessness
Withdrawal, loss of interest in activities
Self-harm (cutting, burning)
Suicidal ideation or threats
Eating disorders, substance abuse

content.text: Note: These are indicators, not proof. A single sign may have an innocent explanation. Patterns of signs or sudden changes warrant attention.

If a student discloses abuse or concern to you: DO: ✅ Listen carefully, calmly, without interrupting ✅ Take it seriously \- believe the student ✅ Reassure \- “Thank you for telling me. You were brave to share this. I’m here to help.” ✅ Record \- Write down what the student said (use their words, not your interpretation) ✅ Report immediately \- Tell DSL/DDSL same day (or immediately if urgent) ✅ Maintain confidentiality \- Share only with DSL and those who need to know DON’T: ❌ Promise absolute confidentiality \- “I can keep this private” (You can’t; you must report) ❌ Investigate \- Don’t question extensively or ask leading questions (“Did your dad hit you?”) ❌ Show shock or disbelief \- Stay calm even if information is disturbing ❌ Delay reporting \- Act same day ❌ Tell the alleged abuser \- This could place the student at greater risk Immediate Safety: If student is in immediate danger:

steps: Secure the student’s safety (separate from alleged abuser if on campus)
Contact DSL immediately (or DDSL if DSL unavailable)
Call emergency services if medical attention or police intervention needed (112 in Indonesia)
Notify General Manager / Principal
content.title: Responding to Disclosures

items: In person (preferred) or by phone/email if urgent
Use Concern/Incident Report Form (Appendix 9-A)
Report same day concern arises; immediately if high risk
content.title: Reporting Procedures

items: Student name, age, grade
Date, time, location of concern/disclosure
Detailed description (facts, not opinions):
What did you observe?
What did the student say (exact words if possible)?
Names of other individuals involved or witnesses
Any immediate actions taken
Your name and signature

content.text: Step 2: DSL Assessment DSL reviews concern and decides:

items: Level 1 (Low risk): Monitor, provide internal support (counseling, check-ins)
Level 2 (Medium risk): Internal support \+ inform parents (unless parental involvement poses risk)
Level 3 (High risk): External referral to authorities required
Severity and immediacy of risk
Pattern of concerns (is this the first report or recurring?)
Student’s wishes (balanced with safety needs)
Legal obligations (mandatory reporting requirements)
Counseling referral
Safety plan (e.g., student avoids specific areas, has check-in schedule with DSL)
Monitoring and follow-up
Police (Unit PPA) \- If criminal offense suspected
P2TP2A (local integrated service center) \- For child protection services
KPAI (Child Protection Commission) \- For advocacy and support
Medical services \- If medical attention needed
Parent is alleged abuser
Informing parents would place student at greater risk
Authorities advise against it (during investigation)
Concern/incident details
Assessment and risk level determination
Actions taken (internal support, referrals, notifications)
Follow-up plan and timeline
Outcomes

content.text: Storage: Secure, confidential file (physical and/or digital with restricted access)
Purpose: These scenarios are based on composite real situations in Indonesian international schools. Use them for:

items: Staff training discussions
Testing understanding of reporting procedures
Identifying early warning signs
content.title: Case Studies: Safeguarding in Action

content.text: Context: Secondary School PE Teacher Scenario: Mr. Budi, a popular PE teacher and football coach, has been at the school for 3 years. Several students have mentioned he's "really cool" and has added them on Instagram. A Grade 9 student, Dina, tells her homeroom teacher: "Coach Budi messaged me last night asking about my weekend plans. He said I could talk to him anytime if I had problems at home. He's so nice!" The homeroom teacher checks Instagram and sees Mr. Budi has 47 current student followers on his personal account. Questions for Discussion:

steps: Is this a safeguarding concern? Why or why not?
What type of concern is this? (Hint: See Section 9.9)
What should the homeroom teacher do immediately?
What should DSL do after receiving this report?
content.title: CASE STUDY 1: The "Friendly" Coach

content.text: CORRECT RESPONSE: Type: Low-Level Concern (Code of Conduct violation \- Section 9.11) Immediate Actions:

steps: Homeroom teacher completes Low-Level Concern Form (Appendix 9-B) \- same day
Report to DSL (not directly to Mr. Budi)
DSL investigates:

items: Checks message content (asks Dina's permission to screenshot)
Interviews Mr. Budi about social media use
Reviews social media policy with Mr. Budi
Checks if other students have similar experiences
Finding: Mr. Budi violated Code of Conduct (Section 9.11: "Do NOT friend or follow students on personal social media")
Action:
Written warning \+ removal of all students from personal social media within 48 hours
Mandatory boundaries training
Logged in Low-Level Concerns Register
If pattern continues or escalates → formal disciplinary process

content.text: Key Learning Points: ✅ Well-intentioned behavior can still be inappropriate ✅ Blurred boundaries create safeguarding risk (grooming often starts with friendly relationship) ✅ Low-level concerns MUST be reported \- pattern recognition prevents escalation

Context: Primary Year 4 Classroom Scenario: It's Monday morning break. Year 4 student Arif (age 9) approaches his teacher, Ms. Sarah, looking upset. The conversation: Arif: "Miss, can I talk to you?" Ms. Sarah: "Of course! What's wrong?" Arif: (crying) "My uncle hit me this weekend. My arm hurts." Ms. Sarah: "Oh no! Can you show me?" (Arif pulls up sleeve \- large bruise on upper arm) Arif: "Please don't tell anyone. He'll be really angry." Questions:

steps: What should Ms. Sarah say RIGHT NOW to Arif?
What should she NOT say?
What are her immediate next steps?
Who needs to be informed and when?
content.title: CASE STUDY 2: Disclosure During Break Time

content.text: CORRECT RESPONSE: What Ms. Sarah Should Say: ✅ "Arif, thank you for telling me. You are very brave." ✅ "I'm really sorry this happened to you. You don't deserve to be hurt." ✅ "I need to share this with [DSL name] because it's their job to make sure you're safe. We only tell people who need to know to help you." ✅ "You're not in trouble. We're going to work together to keep you safe." What Ms. Sarah Should NOT Say: ❌ "I promise I won't tell anyone" (Cannot promise confidentiality) ❌ "Why did he hit you? What did you do?" (Sounds like blaming; not teacher's job to investigate) ❌ "Don't worry, it'll be fine" (Minimizes concern; cannot guarantee outcome) Immediate Actions (Timeline Critical):

steps: Now (10:45 AM): Take Arif to quiet space; call DSL immediately
10:50 AM: DSL sees student; documents disclosure using Arif's exact words
11:00 AM: DSL calls parents to inform (required under Indonesian law)
11:15 AM: DSL assesses: Physical injury visible → HIGH RISK
12:00 PM (latest): DSL reports to Police Unit PPA \+ P2TP2A (24-hour requirement)
12:30 PM: General Manager and Principal informed; student safety plan created
Same day: Ms. Sarah completes Concern Form; DSL starts Case Management Log

items: Concern Form with Arif's exact words
Photos of bruise (with parental consent OR police request)
Police report number (BAP)
Parent communication log
Safety plan (e.g., Arif checks in with DSL daily)

content.text: Key Learning Points: ✅ Disclosures happen unexpectedly \- stay calm ✅ NEVER promise confidentiality ✅ Physical injury \= HIGH RISK \= report to authorities same day ✅ Student safety plan continues even after external report

Context: Secondary School Dormitory Scenario: The dormitory coordinator finds a note in the suggestion box: "Someone in Grade 10 dorm is being bullied. They take her phone at night and make her do their homework. She's scared to tell because they said they'll post embarrassing photos. I don't want to say who I am." Questions:

steps: Is this enough information to act on?
What type of abuse is this?
What should the boarding coordinator do?
How can the school protect the anonymous reporter?
content.title: CASE STUDY 3: The Anonymous Note

items: Type: Peer-on-Peer Abuse (Section 9.13.4) \+ possible cyberbullying (Section 9.13.3)
Risk Level: MEDIUM-HIGH (coercion, threat of image-based abuse)
Enough info? YES \- can identify dorm and year group

steps: Same day: Report to DSL (do NOT investigate alone)
Next 24 hours: DSL \+ dormitory coordinator:

items: Review Grade 10 dorm CCTV (if available) for unusual interactions
Conduct discreet wellbeing check-ins with ALL Grade 10 boarders (normalize, don't single out)
Ask staff: "Have you noticed any changes in Grade 10 students recently?"

steps: Within 48 hours: Identify likely victim through behavioral changes, academic drops
Upon identification:

items: Private conversation with victim (DSL present)
If disclosure confirmed → full investigation (Section 9.13.4 process)

content.text: Protecting Anonymous Reporter: ✅ Do NOT try to identify them (discourages future reporting) ✅ Thank them publicly (assembly): "We received important information that helped keep a student safe" ✅ Remind all students: reporting \= brave, not snitching Investigation Process:

steps: Interview suspected victim (supportive, non-leading questions)
If victim confirms:

items: Immediately secure victim's phone (evidence of coercion/images)
Interview alleged perpetrators separately
Interview witnesses

steps: Outcomes:

items: If substantiated:
Perpetrators: disciplinary action (Section 22) \+ counseling \+ possibly police if distribution of images
Victim: safety plan, counseling, academic support
Dorm supervision increased
If unsubstantiated but concern remains: Continue monitoring

content.text: Key Learning Points: ✅ Anonymous reports ARE actionable ✅ Peer-on-peer abuse often hidden \- requires proactive detection ✅ Image-based threats \= serious (potential criminal offense in Indonesia \- UU ITE)

Context: Primary Year 2 Scenario: DSL reports to police that Year 2 student Lila has suspicious cigarette burns on her legs. Lila told the school counselor "Mama gets angry when I don't study." Police investigate; Dinas Sosial visits the family. Lila's father storms into the Principal's office: "How dare you report us! This is family business! We will sue the school! Remove this from her records or we pull our child out!" Questions:

steps: Did the school act correctly?
How should the Principal respond?
Can parents sue the school for reporting?
What if parents withdraw the child?
content.title: CASE STUDY 4: Parent Pushback

content.text: CORRECT RESPONSE: Did school act correctly? YES \- absolutely.

items: Physical injury on child \= mandatory report under UU 35/2014
24-hour reporting requirement \- no exceptions
School legally REQUIRED to report; failure is criminal offense (Pasal 76)

content.text: Principal's Response (Remain calm, professional, firm): "Mr. [Name], I understand this is distressing. However, I need to explain our legal obligations. Under Indonesian law \- specifically UU No. 35 Tahun 2014 \- schools are REQUIRED to report suspected child abuse to authorities. We don't have the option to not report; failing to report is a criminal offense. Our role is not to investigate or judge your family. Our role is to report concerns so professionals \- police and social services \- can assess the situation. They are trained to determine what support your family may need. We reported in good faith based on visible injuries and Lila's statement. The law protects us from legal action when we report in good faith (Pasal 76). We remain committed to supporting Lila and your family. We can discuss how the school and Dinas Sosial can work together to help." Can parents sue? Unlikely to succeed.

items: Legal protection: UU 35/2014 provides civil immunity for good-faith reporting
School has evidence: Photos, child's statement, professional concern
School CANNOT prevent this (parents' right)
BUT: School MUST inform Dinas Sosial immediately (child may be at increased risk if removed from safe environment)
Document withdrawal reason; maintain safeguarding file

steps: DSL documents entire interaction
GM informs Eduversal Director of Education
School continues cooperation with authorities as required
If Lila returns: monitor closely, liaise with Dinas Sosial

content.text: Key Learning Points: ✅ Schools have legal protection when reporting in good faith ✅ NEVER let parents intimidate you out of proper safeguarding action ✅ "It's family business" is NEVER a reason not to report child abuse ✅ Document everything \- including parents' responses

Context: Year 10 (IGCSE) Scenario: Thursday, 1 PM. The school counselor receives urgent message from Year 11 student Rina's friend: "Miss, I'm really worried. Rina sent me messages last night saying she wants to die. She said she has pills hidden in her dorm. I showed her your number but she won't call. Can you help? Please don't tell her I told you \- she'll hate me." Questions:

steps: What is the immediate priority?
What should counselor do RIGHT NOW?
Who needs to be informed?
How does DSL handle this as safeguarding concern?
content.title: CASE STUDY 5: The Suicidal Student

content.text: CORRECT RESPONSE: Immediate Priority: FIND RINA and ensure her physical safety (prevent suicide attempt) Counselor Actions (Next 15 minutes \- URGENT):

steps: 1:00 PM: Thank the friend; explain must act immediately to keep Rina safe
1:02 PM: Contact DSL \+ boarding house coordinator immediately
1:05 PM: Locate Rina (check timetable \- which class? If absent, check dorm immediately)
1:10 PM: DSL or counselor stays with Rina (DO NOT LEAVE ALONE)
1:15 PM: Counselor conducts suicide risk assessment:

items: "I heard you've been having really hard time. Can you tell me about it?"
"Have you been thinking about hurting yourself?"
"Do you have a plan?" (If YES → HIGH RISK)

content.text: Risk Level Determination: \=== Add Table Here \=== IF HIGH RISK (As in this scenario): Timeline:

steps: 1:20 PM: DSL calls parents: "We need you to come immediately. Rina is safe but needs urgent medical evaluation."
1:25 PM: Search dorm for pills (with another staff member present, document search)
1:30 PM: Parents arrive OR if delayed → DSL takes Rina to hospital emergency
2:00 PM: Rina assessed by psychiatrist
Same day: DSL creates safety plan; GM/Principal informed; incident documented

items: Daily check-ins with counselor
Remove access to potential means (secure medications in clinic)
Buddy system in dorm
Academic accommodations (extensions if needed)
Liaison with external therapist
Parent communication protocol

content.text: Protecting the Reporting Friend: ✅ Explain: "You may have saved Rina's life by telling me" ✅ Rina may be angry initially, but safety comes first ✅ Support friend's wellbeing too (secondary trauma) Key Learning Points: ✅ Suicidal ideation \= IMMEDIATE action required ✅ Specific plan \+ means \= HIGH RISK → hospital evaluation ✅ Never leave suicidal student alone ✅ Mental health is safeguarding issue \- DSL must be involved ✅ Balance confidentiality with safety (safety always wins)

steps: Induction: New teachers discuss Cases 1 & 2
Annual Refresher: All staff discuss Cases 3-5
Role-Specific:
content.title: How to Use These Case Studies:

items: Boarding staff: deep dive on Case 3
Counselors: deep dive on Case 5
Sports coaches: Case 1 plus additional peer pressure scenarios

steps: Read scenario aloud (5 min)
Small groups discuss questions (10 min)
Groups present their responses (10 min)
Facilitator reveals "correct response" \+ discussion (10 min)
Connect to school policy sections (5 min)

items: Training attendance with case studies discussed
Notes from small group discussions
Post-training quiz including case study questions

content.text: Definition: Concerns about an adult’s behavior that do not meet threshold for formal child protection referral but are not consistent with the Code of Conduct (Section 9.11). Examples:

items: Staff member overly familiar with students (excessive compliments, favorites)
Inappropriate use of social media (friending students on personal accounts)
Poor professional boundaries (meeting students alone in private settings without justification)
Comments that make students uncomfortable (not abusive, but unprofessional)
Identify patterns (multiple low-level concerns may indicate higher risk)
Support staff development (coaching, retraining)
Protect staff from false allegations (documentation of context)
Staff member witnesses or receives report of low-level concern
Completes Low-Level Concern Form (Appendix 9-B)
Submits to DSL
DSL speaks with staff member involved (get their perspective)
DSL determines:
No action needed (misunderstanding, no concern warranted)
Informal guidance (remind staff of boundaries, suggest alternate approach)
Formal coaching (if pattern emerging)
Escalate to formal investigation (if concern more serious than initially assessed)
DSL logs concern in Low-Level Concerns Log (anonymized summary)
Reviewed quarterly by GM/Principal
Informs professional development and policy updates
Staff member’s name protected unless formal action required
Purpose is improvement, not punishment
All concerns and disclosures (even if no action taken)
DSL assessments and decisions
Actions taken (internal support, referrals, notifications)
Communication with parents and external agencies
Follow-up notes and outcomes
Case closure (when risk resolved or referred elsewhere)
Use standardized forms (Concern/Incident Report, DSL Case Management Log)
Factual, detailed, dated, signed
Separate safeguarding file for each case (do not mix with student’s academic file)
Secure, locked cabinet in DSL office (physical records)
Encrypted digital files with restricted access (DSL, GM, Principal only)
NOT in general student file or accessible to all staff
Safeguarding records retained until student reaches age 25 (or longer if serious abuse case)
When student transfers, safeguarding records sent to receiving school (with parental consent or legal requirement)
DSL, GM, Principal (authorized personnel only)
External agencies (with appropriate consent or legal mandate)
Parents (limited access; student’s right to confidentiality balanced with parents’ need to know)
Safeguarding records inventory
Access log (who accessed records, when, why)
Annual audit of records compliance

subsections.title: Code of Conduct and Professional Boundaries

content.text: Purpose: Clarify expectations for appropriate behavior and interactions with students; protect both students and staff.

In Interactions with Students: ✅ Treat all students with respect, dignity, and fairness ✅ Use appropriate language (no swearing, sexual innuendo, derogatory remarks) ✅ Maintain physical boundaries (no inappropriate touching; when physical contact needed—e.g., first aid, sports coaching—ensure transparency and appropriateness) ✅ Meet students in visible, public areas (keep doors open or use rooms with windows) ✅ Respond appropriately to student disclosures (listen, report to DSL; don’t investigate) In Communication: ✅ Use school email/communication systems (not personal phone/social media for student contact) ✅ Keep communication professional (no personal conversations, flirting) ✅ Copy parents or colleagues on emails to students when appropriate In Social Media and Technology: ✅ Maintain professional online presence (public profiles should not contain inappropriate content) ❌ Do NOT friend or follow students on personal social media accounts ❌ Do NOT share personal contact information (phone number, personal email, home address) ❌ Do NOT engage in online relationships with students In Discipline: ✅ Use positive, restorative approaches (Section 22) ❌ Do NOT use corporal punishment (illegal in Indonesia and prohibited by school policy) ❌ Do NOT humiliate, shame, or ridicule students Gifts and Favoritism: ✅ Treat all students equitably ❌ Do NOT give personal gifts to individual students (school-funded awards are appropriate) ❌ Do NOT show favoritism or develop special relationships with students One-to-One Situations: ✅ When necessary (tutoring, counseling, music lessons), inform colleagues and keep doors open or use visible spaces ❌ Avoid being alone with a student in a locked, isolated area Transporting Students: ❌ Do NOT transport students in personal vehicles (except in emergencies or with GM approval and parental consent) Evidence:

items: Signed Code of Conduct acknowledgment (all staff, annually)
Code of Conduct training (included in induction and annual refresher)
content.title: All Staff Must:

content.text: Link to Section 22: Full student conduct and discipline procedures are outlined in Section 22. Key principles include:

steps: Progressive discipline (prevention → intervention → consequences)
Restorative conferences for peer-on-peer harm
Behavior monitoring and parent involvement
Appeals process

content.text: Definition: Allegation that a staff member, volunteer, or contractor has:

items: Behaved in a way that harmed or posed risk of harm to a child
Committed a criminal offense against or related to a child
Behaved in a way that indicates unsuitability to work with children
Any allegation against staff reported immediately to Principal (not to DSL if DSL is the subject of allegation)
If allegation is against GM, report to Eduversal Director of Education
content.title: Immediate Actions

content.text: Step 2: Assess Risk and Take Precautionary Measures

items: Principal (with HR and legal advice) determines whether staff member should be:
Continue working (if allegation clearly unfounded)
Suspended with pay pending investigation (if risk to students or integrity of investigation)
Reassigned to non-student-facing role temporarily
Severity of allegation
Safety of students
Fair treatment of staff member (presumption of innocence until proven otherwise)
Conducted by GM or external investigator (if conflict of interest)
Interview alleged victim, witnesses, accused staff member
Review evidence (documents, communications, CCTV if applicable)
Maintain confidentiality (only those with need to know)
If allegation involves criminal behavior, report to police (mandatory under Indonesian law)
Cooperate fully with police investigation
School investigation may run parallel but should not interfere with criminal investigation
Disciplinary action up to termination of employment
Report to authorities (police, teacher licensing board if applicable)
Ensure alleged victim receives support
Review safeguarding practices to prevent recurrence
Clear staff member’s record
Provide support to staff member (reputational repair, counseling if needed)
Address malicious false allegations appropriately (student discipline if warranted; not punitive if genuine mistake)
Return staff member to duties with appropriate support
Investigation completed as quickly as possible (typically within 4-6 weeks)
Staff member and alleged victim kept informed of progress
Investigation file (secure, confidential)
Notes from interviews and evidence review
Decision documentation with rationale
Communication records with staff member and authorities

content.text: Boarding environments require additional safeguarding measures due to 24/7 student care. Dormitory Supervision:

items: Gender-appropriate staff (male staff for male dorms, female staff for female dorms)
Minimum ratio: 1 staff per 20 students (overnight)
Clear sleeping arrangements (staff separate from students but accessible)
content.title: 9.13.1 Boarding Schools (If Applicable)

items: Regular checks/patrols (documented)
Students know how to contact staff in emergencies
Secure entry/exit (prevent unauthorized access and students leaving unsupervised)
Students have lockable personal storage
Bathroom facilities ensure privacy (locks, cubicles)
Room searches (if necessary) conducted with witness and student present
Regular communication with families
Pastoral support for adjustment difficulties
Peer mentoring (older students support new boarders)
All boarding staff receive specialized training in residential safeguarding
Training covers: nighttime disclosures, managing homesickness, appropriate physical contact (comforting distressed students), boundaries in informal settings
Boarding-specific safeguarding policy addendum
Dorm supervision schedules and check logs
Boarding staff training records
Parent communication records

content.text: Safeguarding Risk Assessment (Mandatory for All Trips): Before Trip:

items: Complete Trip Risk Assessment Form (Appendix 9-C)
Identify safeguarding risks specific to destination and activity
Plan mitigation measures (supervision ratios, emergency contacts, buddy systems)
GM/Principal approval required before trip proceeds
content.title: 9.13.2 School Trips and Off-Site Activities

items: Supervision ratios: Minimum 1:10 (1 adult per 10 students); higher ratio for younger students, higher-risk activities, or students with additional needs
Gender balance: Mixed-gender trips should have both male and female staff
Student medical and safeguarding information: Trip leaders carry confidential list of students with medical conditions, allergies, IEPs, safeguarding concerns
Accommodation: Overnight trips require careful accommodation planning (student sleeping areas separate from staff but accessible; gender-appropriate arrangements)
Communication: Students and parents have emergency contact numbers; staff have student emergency contacts
Staff maintain vigilance (monitor student wellbeing, peer interactions, safety)
Regular roll calls/head counts
Students know how to contact staff if concerns arise
Staff follow same Code of Conduct and boundaries as on campus
Debrief with students (any concerns or incidents?)
Complete incident/concern forms if safeguarding issues arose
Evaluate trip and risk assessment (lessons learned for future trips)
Trip risk assessments (all trips, past 3 years)
Trip leader training completion records
Incident reports from trips (if any)
Parent consent forms (including consent for emergency medical treatment)

content.text: Digital Safeguarding Policy (closely linked to Section 21: Digital Learning) Risks:

items: Cyberbullying (social media harassment, spreading rumors, sharing embarrassing photos/videos)
Online grooming (predators posing as peers, building trust to exploit)
Exposure to inappropriate content (violence, pornography, hate speech)
Sexting (sharing or being pressured to share sexual images)
Privacy violations (hacking, identity theft, sharing personal information)
Screen addiction and mental health impacts
content.title: 9.13.3 Online Safety

items: Age-appropriate content filtering on school network
Blocked sites: pornography, violence, gambling, extremism
Monitored access (logs reviewed regularly; alerts for high-risk searches)
All students and staff sign AUP annually (Section 21)
Clear expectations for appropriate online behavior
Consequences for misuse
School-issued devices (Chromebooks, tablets) have monitoring software
Students’ personal devices on school WiFi subject to acceptable use expectations
Online safety lessons (Primary through Upper Secondary; age-appropriate)
Topics: privacy settings, recognizing grooming, digital citizenship, reporting concerns, managing online reputation
Students know how to report cyberbullying or online concerns (to DSL, IT staff, or anonymously)
Staff trained to recognize and respond to online safeguarding issues
Parent workshops on online safety (managing screen time, monitoring apps, privacy settings)
Resources shared via newsletters, website
Filtered internet and monitoring system documentation
AUP signed records (100% students and staff)
Online safety curriculum maps and lesson plans
Parent workshop attendance and feedback

content.text: Recognition: Abuse perpetrated by students against other students. Includes:

items: Physical violence (hitting, pushing, fighting)
Bullying (verbal, social exclusion, cyberbullying)
Sexual harassment (unwanted touching, sexual comments, requests for sexual images)
Sexual violence (assault, coercion)
Initiation/hazing rituals
Prejudice-based abuse (racism, homophobia, ableism)
content.title: 9.13.4 Peer-on-Peer Abuse

content.text: School Response: Zero Tolerance for Peer-on-Peer Abuse:

items: Clear message: This behavior is unacceptable, harmful, and has consequences
Victims supported; perpetrators held accountable; restorative approaches used (Section 22)
DSL investigates allegations (interviews victim, alleged perpetrator, witnesses)
Assess risk to victim and other students
Determine appropriate intervention
Safety plan (avoid contact with perpetrator if needed)
Counseling
Academic accommodations if needed (e.g., trauma affecting concentration)
Parental involvement (kept informed, involved in support plan)
Age-appropriate consequences (disciplinary action per student conduct policy; Section 22)
Education (understanding impact of behavior, empathy development)
Monitoring and support (address underlying issues: anger management, peer pressure, own trauma)
Involve parents
If sexual assault or serious violence, report to police (mandatory under Indonesian law)
Consider external support for perpetrator (counseling, therapeutic intervention)
Anti-bullying curriculum and campaigns
Peer mediation and conflict resolution training
Student leadership in promoting respectful culture (peer ambassadors, clubs)
Peer-on-peer abuse incidents log (anonymized summaries)
Victim support plans
Perpetrator intervention plans
Anti-bullying program documentation (curriculum, events, surveys)

content.text: Integration with Safeguarding: Mental health concerns (self-harm, suicidal ideation, severe anxiety/depression) are safeguarding issues requiring DSL involvement. Warning Signs:

items: Self-harm (cutting, burning, hitting self)
Suicidal thoughts or threats (“I wish I was dead,” “Everyone would be better off without me”)
Sudden withdrawal from friends, activities
Drastic changes in behavior, appearance, academic performance
Risky behaviors (substance abuse, reckless actions)
content.title: 9.13.5 Mental Health and Wellbeing

content.text: Response: Immediate Risk (Suicidal Ideation or Active Self-Harm):

items: Do not leave student alone
Contact DSL immediately
Call parents/guardians
Seek emergency medical/psychiatric care if needed (take student to hospital; call crisis hotline)
Counseling referral (school counselor or external therapist)
Safety plan (coping strategies, support network, crisis contacts)
Academic accommodations (if mental health affecting schoolwork)
Regular check-ins (DSL, counselor, trusted teachers)
Inform parents (except in rare cases where parental involvement poses risk)
Collaborate on support plan
Provide resources (therapists, support groups, crisis hotlines)
Mental health awareness training (recognizing signs, responding compassionately, reducing stigma)
Suicide prevention training (assessing risk, intervening, connecting to resources)
Wellbeing curriculum (stress management, mindfulness, help-seeking)
Reduce stigma (mental health awareness campaigns, normalize counseling)
Accessible counseling services (students know how to access; appointments not overly delayed)
Mental health support plans (confidential)
Counseling referral records
Mental health training completion records
Wellbeing programs documentation

content.text: Purpose: Empower staff to report concerns about safeguarding practices, leadership failures, or cover-ups without fear of retaliation.

items: Safeguarding concerns not addressed by DSL or GM
Leadership failing to follow safeguarding procedures
Pressure to not report concerns or downplay incidents
Unsafe practices (inadequate supervision, lack of background checks)
Cover-up of abuse or misconduct
content.title: What to Report:

steps: Report to GM (if concern about DSL or other staff)
Report to Chief of Academics (if concern about GM or school-level leadership)
Report to Board Chair (if concern about Director of Education or systemic network issue)
content.title: How to Report:

content.text: External Reporting (If Internal Channels Fail or Unsafe):

items: Indonesian authorities: KPAI, police, Ministry of Education
Cambridge International (if Cambridge Centre-related)
CIS/WASC (if accreditation-related)
No retaliation: Staff who report concerns in good faith protected from disciplinary action, termination, or harassment
Confidentiality: Reporter’s identity protected to extent possible (may be disclosed if investigation requires)
Investigation: All whistleblowing concerns investigated promptly and impartially
Follow-up: Reporter informed of outcome (to extent confidentiality allows)
content.title: Protections:

content.text: False or malicious reports made to damage reputation or avoid accountability may result in disciplinary action. Evidence:

items: Whistleblowing policy (in staff handbook)
Whistleblowing reports log (confidential, restricted access)
Investigation outcomes
content.title: Malicious Reporting:

items: National child protection commission
Advocacy, policy development, public awareness
School contacts for guidance on complex cases
content.title: Indonesian Child Protection Agencies

content.text: P2TP2A (Pusat Pelayanan Terpadu Pemberdayaan Perempuan dan Anak):

items: Local integrated service centers (provincial and district level)
Provide direct services: counseling, legal aid, shelter for victims
School refers cases needing intensive intervention

content.text: Police (Unit PPA \- Unit Perlindungan Perempuan dan Anak):

items: Women and Children Protection Unit
Investigate criminal offenses against children
School reports criminal abuse (physical, sexual) and cooperates with investigations
Provide social welfare support (housing, financial assistance for families in crisis)
School refers cases of severe neglect or family dysfunction
Medical examinations for suspected abuse (forensic evidence if sexual abuse)
Emergency care for injuries
content.title: Medical and Mental Health Services

items: Therapeutic intervention for trauma, mental health crises
School refers students needing specialized care beyond school counseling
Yayasan Pulih: Trauma counseling and recovery services
Lentera Sintas Indonesia: Support for sexual violence survivors
Save the Children Indonesia: Child protection advocacy and programs
content.title: NGOs and Community Organizations

items: Guest speakers for student/parent awareness sessions
Training for staff
Referral pathway for specialized support

content.text: Schools may establish formal partnerships (MOUs) with key agencies:

items: Define roles and responsibilities
Establish referral protocols
Facilitate information sharing (within legal/ethical boundaries)
content.title: Memoranda of Understanding (MOUs)

items: Contact list for external agencies (kept current)
MOUs with partner agencies (if applicable)
Referral records (anonymized summaries)
Training provided by external agencies

content.text: Purpose: Systematically review safeguarding practices and identify areas for improvement.
When: End of academic year (May-June) Who: GM, Principal, DSL, with support from Eduversal Director of Education (may include external consultant) What to Review: 1. Policy and Procedures:

items: Is Safeguarding Policy current and compliant with Indonesian law, CIS/WASC, Cambridge?
Are procedures clear and followed consistently?
Any updates needed based on new regulations or best practices?
content.title: Annual Audit Process

items: Did 100% of staff complete annual training?
Was training effective? (Evaluate using post-training surveys, knowledge checks)
What specialized training is needed for next year?
How many concerns reported? (Trend analysis: increasing, decreasing, stable?)
Types of concerns (abuse, neglect, bullying, mental health, online safety)
Response times (Were concerns addressed promptly?)
Outcomes (Were students supported? Were cases resolved?)
Is Single Central Register complete and current?
Were all required checks completed for new hires?
Any gaps in process?
Student surveys: Do students feel safe? Do they know how to report concerns?
Parent surveys: Are parents confident in school’s safeguarding measures?
Focus groups or interviews (selected students, parents) for deeper insight
Staff survey: Do staff understand safeguarding procedures? Do they feel supported in safeguarding role?
Suggestions for improvement?
Feedback from police, social services, counselors on school’s collaboration and referrals
Safety audit: Secure entry/exit, CCTV coverage, lighting, safe play areas
Any physical safety hazards?

content.text: Develop improvement action plan based on audit findings:

items: Specific actions (e.g., “Update Code of Conduct to address social media boundaries”)
Responsible person (DSL, GM, IT Manager)
Timeline (by start of next year, by mid-year)
Resources needed (budget, training provider)
content.title: Action Plan

items: Quarterly check-ins (SLT meetings)
Mid-year audit update
Local Yayasan
Eduversal Board of Trustees (via Director of Education)
content.title: Annual Safeguarding Report

items: Summary of safeguarding activities (training, awareness campaigns)
Incident data (anonymized statistics, trends)
Audit findings and action plan
Successes and challenges
Resource needs
Annual safeguarding audit report
Action plan with progress tracking
Stakeholder survey results
Annual report to governance bodies
Safeguarding and Child Protection Policy (current version, reviewed annually)
Code of Conduct for staff (signed by all staff annually)
Anti-Bullying Policy (student-facing, age-appropriate language)
Online Safety Policy (linked to Section 21)
Whistleblowing Policy
Boarding Safeguarding Addendum (if applicable)
content.title: Policies and Procedures:

items: Single Central Register (SCR) \- complete and current, including:
All safeguarding training completion dates
Certificate issue and expiry dates (2-year validity tracked)
Training type and frequency by role:
Educators Level 1: Every 2 years
Support Staff (60-90 min): Every 2 years
Volunteers (30-45 min): Every 2 years
DSL/DDSL (2-3 days): Every 2 years
Board/Leaders (1 hour): Every 2 years
Annual Refresher (all staff): Every year (90 minutes)
Individual staff files (ID, qualifications, references, background checks, digitally verifiable training certificates with verification codes, Code of Conduct)
Induction training materials and attendance records
Annual refresher training materials and attendance records (100% completion)
Role-specific training records:
DSL/DDSL: Advanced 2-3 day certification
Board/Leaders: 1-hour governance training completion
Support Staff: 60-90 minute specialized course certificates
Volunteers: 30-45 minute course certificates
Trip Leaders: Trip safeguarding training completion
Boarding Staff: Residential safeguarding certification
IT Staff: Online safety training records
Training compliance dashboard showing:
Percentage of staff with current certificates
Upcoming certificate expirations
Training gaps by role
CPD certification documentation for all courses
Parent tutorial completion tracking and resource access logs
content.title: Recruitment and Training:

items: Concern/Incident Report Forms (Appendix 9-A) \- all submitted reports
DSL Case Management Log (secure, confidential)
Low-Level Concerns Log (Appendix 9-B)
Safeguarding records for individual students (secure, confidential files)
External agency referral records (police, social services)
content.title: Incident Management:

items: Safeguarding curriculum maps (PSHE, PPKn, Wellbeing lessons)
Student safety education lesson plans and materials
Parent handbook with safeguarding section (English and Bahasa Indonesia)
Parent workshop materials and attendance records
content.title: Student and Parent Awareness:

items: Trip risk assessments (Appendix 9-C) \- all trips, past 3 years
Boarding safeguarding risk assessment (if applicable)
Online safety risk assessment
Physical environment safety audits
content.title: Risk Assessments:

items: Annual safeguarding audit reports (past 3 years)
Action plans with progress tracking
Student, parent, and staff survey results (safeguarding-related questions)
Annual safeguarding report to Yayasan and Eduversal Board
content.title: Monitoring and Improvement:

items: Board meeting minutes addressing safeguarding (minimum annually)
Yayasan meeting minutes addressing safeguarding
DSL appointment letter and job description
DDSL appointment letters
content.title: Governance:

items: Law No. 35 of 2014 (Child Protection): Mandatory reporting, safe school environment, anti-violence
Law No. 17 of 2016 (Digital Child Protection): Online safety measures
Ministry of Education regulations: Child-friendly schools (Sekolah Ramah Anak)
content.title: Indonesian Legal Requirements:

items: Standard 7B: Health and Safety (safeguarding policies, safer recruitment, training)
Standard 3C: School Culture (safe, respectful environment; anti-bullying)
Standard 8A: Operational Systems (policies, procedures, record keeping)
content.title: CIS Standards:

items: Category E: School Culture and Support for Student Personal and Academic Growth
E1: Safe, respectful, equitable learning environment
E2: Student support systems (counseling, intervention for at-risk students)
E3: Policies and practices that ensure student wellbeing
content.title: WASC Categories:

items: Safeguarding policy and procedures (UK-aligned)
Safer recruitment for all exam personnel
Secure handling of student data
Reporting of serious incidents affecting exams
content.title: Cambridge Centre Requirements:

items: Article 19: Protection from all forms of violence, abuse, neglect
Article 34: Protection from sexual exploitation
Article 12: Right to be heard (student voice in safeguarding)
content.title: UN Convention on the Rights of the Child (UNCRC):

content.text: The following templates support implementation of this safeguarding policy and are provided in the Handbook Appendices (Section 19):

items: Appendix 9-A: Concern/Incident Report Form
Appendix 9-B: Low-Level Concern Form and Log
Appendix 9-C: Trip Safeguarding Risk Assessment Template
Appendix 9-D: Code of Conduct Acknowledgment Form
Appendix 9-E: DSL Case Management Log Template
Appendix 9-F: Safeguarding Training Attendance Record
Appendix 9-G: Annual Safeguarding Audit Checklist
Appendix 9-H: Student Safety Plan Template
Appendix 9-I: Parent Information Sheet (Safeguarding Policy Summary \- English and Bahasa Indonesia)

content.text: When: End of each academic year (May-June), as part of annual safeguarding audit Who: DSL leads review, with input from GM, Principal, SLT, and Eduversal Director of Education What to Review:

items: Compliance with current Indonesian law and international standards
Effectiveness based on audit findings
Incorporation of lessons learned from incidents
Updates to reflect new risks (e.g., emerging online threats)
Stakeholder feedback (staff, students, parents)
Training effectiveness and completion rates:
Percentage of staff with current (non-expired) certificates
Training evaluation scores and feedback
Correlation between training and incident reporting rates
Gaps in training coverage by role or campus
Need for new specialized training topics
content.title: Annual Review

items: Reviewed policy submitted to GM for approval
Significant changes submitted to Eduversal Board and local Yayasan for approval
Updated policy distributed to all staff; key changes communicated to students and parents

content.text: When: January (mid-academic year) Purpose: Quick review to address any urgent issues or regulatory changes
content.title: Mid-Year Check

items: New Indonesian law or regulation requires changes
Serious incident reveals gap in policy or procedures
External audit (Ministry, Cambridge, CIS/WASC) identifies deficiencies
content.title: Emergency Updates

items: Policy review meeting notes
Stakeholder feedback summaries
Updated policy versions with change log
Board/Yayasan approval records

subsections.title: Conclusion: Commitment to Every Child’s Safety

content.text: Safeguarding is not a checklist—it is a culture that permeates every aspect of school life. Every member of the Eduversal Partner Schools community shares responsibility for creating an environment where:

items: Children feel safe, valued, and heard
Adults are vigilant, responsive, and accountable
Families are partners in protection
Systems are robust, transparent, and continuously improving

content.text: Our Promise: We will do everything in our power to protect the children in our care. When concerns arise, we will act swiftly, compassionately, and professionally. We will learn from every incident to strengthen our practices. And we will never compromise on the safety and wellbeing of our students. This is our sacred duty. This is our commitment.
